stories.headline: Avalanche GM Chris MacFarland departs for Nashville; Joe Sakic resumes general manager duties
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche announced Tuesday that general manager Chris MacFarland has departed the organization to take a larger role with the Nashville Predators. Kroenke Sports & Entertainment Vice Chairman Josh Kroenke released a statement thanking MacFarland for his contributions, including his role in the franchise's 2022 Stanley Cup championship.

Avalanche President Joe Sakic will resume general manager duties for the foreseeable future, including through this month's NHL Draft and the start of the league year. Sakic previously served as the team's EVP/GM for nine seasons from 2013-14 to 2021-22, leading the franchise to the 2022 Cup title and winning the Jim Gregory General Manager of the Year Award that summer.

Sakic is the only individual in NHL history to win a Stanley Cup as both captain and GM with the same organization, having captained Colorado to championships in 1996 and 2001. The team said Sakic will be made available to the media when he returns to Denver next week.

stories.headline: Colorado Republicans clash in heated gubernatorial primary debate at University of Denver
stories.summary:
The three Republican candidates for Colorado governor — state Rep. Scott Bottoms, state Sen. Barb Kirkmeyer, and missionary leader Victor Marx — faced off in a contentious 60-minute televised debate Tuesday night at the University of Denver, sponsored by 9News, The Denver Gazette, and Colorado Politics. The event is expected to be the only primary debate to include Marx, the race's fundraising leader, who has declined to participate in other GOP gubernatorial debates.

All three candidates agreed that Colorado has deteriorated under eight years of Democratic governance, though the debate quickly turned personal. Bottoms reiterated previous claims that Marx is a 'con man' and 'corrupt,' while Kirkmeyer said she would not support Marx if he won the nomination. Marx, positioning himself as a political outsider, responded to the attacks by saying, 'You're mean.'

Primary ballots are set to begin going out to Colorado voters on June 8 and are due back to county clerks by 7 p.m. on June 30. A companion debate featuring Democratic candidates U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet and Attorney General Phil Weiser is scheduled for Thursday evening.

stories.headline: Polis signs election law extending mail ballot window, adding safeguards against federal interference
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed House Bill 1113 into law Monday, extending Colorado's mail ballot window by seven days and establishing new protections against potential federal interference in state elections. Under the new law, election officials may begin mailing ballots 29 days before an election, and drop boxes must accept ballots for 22 days before Election Day.

The legislation also grants the governor authority to declare an emergency when the state cannot strictly comply with its own election code and establishes a committee that could be convened to draft new rules ensuring proper election administration. The bill passed along party lines, with Republicans voting against it.

The law comes amid concerns that President Trump may attempt to interfere with November midterm elections. Trump signed an executive order in March to restrict mail-in voting, and the U.S. Postal Service recently proposed requiring states to turn over lists of voters who received mail-in ballots. The new law will not affect this year's June 30 primaries.

stories.headline: Polis appeals court order blocking compliance with ICE subpoena targeting undocumented children's sponsors
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis has filed a notice of appeal with the Colorado Court of Appeals seeking to overturn a Denver district court order that prohibited him from directing state employees to comply with a federal Immigration and Customs Enforcement subpoena. The subpoena seeks personal information about sponsors of undocumented children held in state labor department records.

Denver District Court Judge A. Bruce Jones ruled in April that ICE's latest subpoena appeared to be a revised version of an earlier one, updated with language referencing a criminal investigation to sidestep a state law prohibiting information-sharing with ICE outside of criminal probes. Polis argues that the criminal investigation language in the subpoena is sufficient to override state law.

The legal battle began nearly 12 months ago when a former state employee sued to block the governor from turning over the sponsors' information. As of Monday, the state has spent more than $215,000 in outside legal fees defending the governor and attempting to comply with the subpoenas, according to figures obtained from the attorney general's office.

stories.headline: Update: Driver in deadly Highlands Ranch sidewalk crash had suspended license from prior DUI
stories.summary:
New details have emerged in Monday's fatal Highlands Ranch crash in which a driver allegedly steered a Ford F-150 onto a sidewalk and struck three pedestrians on Wildcat Reserve Parkway. The accused driver, Adam Robert Bauserman, 28, was operating the vehicle with a suspended license stemming from a DUI in April 2025, during which his blood alcohol content was recorded above 0.20 — more than twice the legal limit.

One victim, identified in court documents as Corrine More, 35, was killed. Two others were hospitalized with serious injuries including a brain bleed and spinal fractures. Witnesses told police they believed the action was intentional given the truck's high speed before it jumped the curb.

After the crash, the truck re-entered the roadway, made a U-turn past the crash site, and Bauserman was arrested approximately five and a half miles away. He appeared in court Tuesday morning and told officers at the scene that he believed he had hit someone. He is due back in court Friday.

stories.headline: Officers shoot, wound suspect in Denver following construction vehicle theft; 6th Avenue and Santa Fe closed
stories.summary:
A 35-year-old man suspected of stealing a flatbed work truck was shot by law enforcement officers Tuesday at a Sinclair gas station at 620 Santa Fe Drive in Denver's Lincoln Park neighborhood, authorities said. A Colorado State Patrol trooper and an Adams County Sheriff's deputy are believed to have fired the shots. The suspect was transported to a hospital in stable condition, and no officers were injured.

The company that owned the truck reported the theft at approximately 9 a.m. from an undisclosed location in eastern Adams County. A GPS tracking device in the vehicle led the Colorado Metropolitan Auto Theft Task Force — which includes the Denver Police Department, Colorado State Patrol, and Adams County Sheriff's Office — to the gas station, where the suspect was confronted inside.

Colorado State Patrol Chief Matthew Packard said at a news conference that investigators believe the suspect was armed at the time of the encounter but declined to specify what prompted the shooting. The Denver Police Department is leading the investigation as part of its critical incident protocol, joined by the Colorado Bureau of Investigation.

stories.headline: Denver City Council approves $4.5M purchase of former state office building for affordable housing
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council voted Monday to approve a $4.5 million purchase of the former Colorado Department of Labor and Employment office building at 251 E. 12th Ave. in Capitol Hill, marking the first use of Vibrant Denver Bond funds for affordable housing. Denver Assessor records place the appraised value of the three-story, 91,470-square-foot building — constructed in 1957 — at more than $18 million, suggesting the city obtained a significant discount.

The city plans to either lease the property to a developer or sell it outright to a party that can convert it into affordable housing. Voters approved the nearly $1 billion Vibrant Denver Bond last year, with $45 million allocated for acquiring or preparing properties for affordable housing purposes.

City officials said the Capitol Hill location is well-suited for affordable housing given proximity to schools, childcare, parks, grocery stores, and public transit. The sole dissenting vote came from Councilmember Flor Alvidrez, who cited a lack of detail on the feasibility of the building's conversion and the specific type of affordable housing to be developed. The city plans to close on the purchase by the end of the month.

stories.headline: Denver-based Voyager Technologies to acquire moon lander company Astrobotic for $300 million
stories.summary:
Denver-based Voyager Technologies announced plans to acquire Pittsburgh-based Astrobotic Technology for up to $300 million, combining Astrobotic's lunar landing capabilities with Voyager's existing space infrastructure portfolio. The deal represents a significant consolidation in the commercial space sector.

Astrobotic has been a prominent player in NASA's Commercial Lunar Payload Services program, having developed the Peregrine lunar lander. The acquisition would give Voyager Technologies a more comprehensive set of capabilities spanning both space infrastructure and surface operations on the Moon.

The deal underscores the growing role of Denver-area companies in the commercial space industry, as Colorado continues to establish itself as a major hub for aerospace and space technology firms.

stories.headline: Polis signs bill codifying disability rights for students with 504 plans, though funding remains uncertain
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed Senate Bill 125 into law Friday, codifying the federal civil rights of students with disabilities into Colorado state law and directing the Colorado Department of Education to establish a process for investigating discrimination complaints from families of students with 504 plans. The legislation was driven partly by major cuts to the federal Office for Civil Rights under the Trump administration, which closed seven of its 12 regional offices and dismissed nearly 300 employees.

The new law will also give families an avenue to report schools that fail to adequately address bullying and will hold school officials accountable for retaliating against parents advocating for their children's rights. However, Polis raised questions about how the state will fund the effort — the department would need more than $650,000 to hire three staff members responsible for investigating complaints.

Whether Colorado can afford the program in a tight budget year remains uncertain, as the bill relies on gifts, grants, and donations to cover costs. Disability Law Colorado co-legal director Emily Harvey, who helped write the legislation, said the new law is an important step as the federal government reduces its civil rights enforcement role.

stories.headline: Colorado's first West Nile virus case of 2026 confirmed in Jefferson County resident
stories.summary:
State and local health officials confirmed Tuesday that a Jefferson County resident has tested positive for West Nile virus, marking the first confirmed human case in Colorado for the 2026 season. The case comes earlier than the typical mid-July start of West Nile season, though health officials note cases can be identified as early as May.

Jefferson County Environmental Health Director James Rada said recent precipitation may create mosquito breeding conditions despite a dry winter. While most people infected with West Nile experience no symptoms, those aged 55 and older, immunocompromised individuals, and people with chronic health conditions face the greatest risk of serious illness.

In 2025, Colorado reported 286 West Nile cases, with approximately half requiring hospitalization and 18 resulting in death. Health officials recommend eliminating standing water around homes, using insect repellent, wearing long sleeves and pants, and avoiding outdoor activity at dusk and dawn when mosquitoes are most active.

stories.headline: RTD rider satisfaction climbs to 82-87%, but systemwide ridership remains 38% below pre-pandemic levels
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District released its annual customer and community survey Monday showing 82% satisfaction among bus riders and 87% among rail riders in 2026, continuing an upward trend in customer experience scores. RTD CEO Debra A. Johnson credited ongoing initiatives focused on creating a welcoming transit environment and improving the customer experience.

Despite the satisfaction gains, systemwide ridership remains approximately 38% below 2019 levels, and federal census data shows public transit's share of Denver-area work commutes has fallen well below pre-pandemic figures. Critics argue the positive feedback from existing riders does not reflect broader regional sentiment in a metro area where roughly 96% of all commutes rely on personal vehicles.

The survey results come as RTD advances bus rapid transit projects on corridors including Colorado Boulevard. Opponents such as former RTD board chairman Jon Caldara contend the projects reduce overall vehicle capacity, while supporters including CDOT's BRT program manager Ryan Knowles argue the improvements benefit all road users through safety, congestion relief, and transit signal priority technology.

stories.headline: Denver gas prices drop 42 cents in one week, now averaging $4.19 per gallon
stories.summary:
Gasoline prices in the Denver metro area fell 42 cents over the past week to an average of $4.19 per gallon, according to fuel tracking platform GasBuddy, which surveyed more than 844 local stations. Prices are also down 26 cents compared to a month ago, providing relief after elevated costs tied to the Iran conflict earlier this year.

The decline mirrors a national trend, with GasBuddy reporting drops in all 50 states and 15 states now averaging below $4 per gallon. AAA spokesperson Skyler McKinley attributed the local improvement partly to natural post-Memorial Day price declines and stable Colorado production levels, noting the state is now roughly in line with the national average after spending months above it.

Experts caution that prices could rebound quickly depending on developments surrounding the Strait of Hormuz. 'Crude oil prices are a moving target based entirely off what traders read in the international headlines,' McKinley said. For context, the Denver average a year ago was approximately $3.04 per gallon.

joke.joke: I gave my cat a bath the other day. They love it. He sat there, he enjoyed it. It was fun for me. The fur would stick to my tongue, but other than that...

topStory.headline: California Governor's Race Too Close to Call as Hilton, Becerra and Steyer Lead Crowded Field
topStory.summary:
California's wide-open gubernatorial primary remained unresolved Wednesday morning with roughly half of all expected votes counted, as three candidates held the top positions in a race that will ultimately send only two contenders to the November general election. Former Fox News host and Republican Steve Hilton led with approximately 27 percent of the vote, followed closely by Democrat and former U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Xavier Becerra at 26 percent and billionaire Democratic activist Tom Steyer at 20 percent. Republican Riverside County Sheriff Chad Bianco was the only other candidate in double digits, at 11 percent, but trailed the top three by a wide margin.

Two Democratic candidates conceded shortly after polls closed Tuesday night — San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan and former Los Angeles Mayor Antonio Villaraigosa — clearing some of the uncertainty in the field. Steyer declined to concede, telling supporters in San Francisco that he intended to wait for a full vote count. Becerra, speaking to supporters on election night, cast himself as an underdog who had been counted out and outspent throughout the race, saying, 'The underdog stayed in the fight.'

The race to succeed term-limited Democratic Governor Gavin Newsom unfolded against a backdrop of unusual political uncertainty. No dominant candidate emerged to consolidate support in a state where Democrats hold nearly a two-to-one voter registration advantage, in part because several prominent Democrats — including U.S. Senator Alex Padilla and Attorney General Rob Bonta — declined to enter the race. The field ultimately swelled to 61 candidates on a single primary ballot under California's jungle primary system, which allows all candidates regardless of party to compete together with only the top two finishers advancing.

In other California races, incumbent Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass advanced to a November runoff after failing to secure an outright majority, and will face either reality television personality Spencer Pratt or City Council member Nithya Raman for her seat. In the race to fill retiring Rep. Nancy Pelosi's House seat, San Francisco County Supervisor Connie Chan and state Sen. Scott Wiener both advanced, setting up a Democrat-versus-Democrat general election contest. Progressive activist Saikat Chakrabarti, a former chief of staff to Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, did not advance.

A final outcome in the governor's race is not expected for days or possibly weeks, given California's extensive use of mail-in voting and the state's rigorous ballot verification process. Many Democratic voters are known to submit mail ballots close to or on Election Day, further delaying the count. Political observers noted that Republican candidates in California have historically performed better on election night than after mail ballots are fully tabulated, a dynamic that could still shift the standings among the top contenders.
